article_metadata.title: Design and evaluation of effervescent floating tablets based on hydroxyethyl cellulose and sodium alginate using pentoxifylline as a model drug

sections_locations.abstract: First page: aim, materials, key methods, main results, conclusion. [file:1]
sections_locations.methods: Subsections under 'Materials and methods': Granulation; Powder mixtures and granules evaluation; DSC; FT-IR; Tablets preparation; Evaluation of tablets; Quality control tests; Floating capacity; Swelling/erosion; In vitro drug release; Release data modeling; Statistical analysis. [file:1]
sections_locations.results_and_discussion: Headed 'Results and discussion', including moisture content, DSC, FT-IR, tablet properties, floating, swelling/erosion, dissolution, release modeling. [file:1]

properties_reported.density: described as high density (numerical value not given)

sodium_alginate.particle_size_used: ≤350 µm (to overcome compression problem when 180 µm cut was used) [file:1]

sodium_bicarbonate.true_density: 2.173 g/cm3 (cited property, used to discuss effects on density/porosity) [file:1]

experimental_design_for_optimization.overall_goal: Design effervescent floating gastro-retentive matrix tablets with sustained release using hydroxyethyl cellulose–sodium alginate binary matrix and pentoxifylline as model drug; evaluate effects of wet granulation, sodium bicarbonate level (10% vs 20% w/w), and tablet hardness on density, floating, swelling, erosion, and drug release. [file:1]

independent_variables.name: tablet_hardness
levels: C: 59–64 N (only from powder mixture; granules could not be compressed to C)
independent_variables.section: Tablets preparation; Evaluation of tablets; Table 3. [file:1]

independent_variables.name: granulation_state
levels: granules (wet granulation with 0.5% w/w water, oven-dried)
independent_variables.section: Granulation; Powder mixtures and granules evaluation; multiple result tables. [file:1]

independent_variables.name: floating_vs_nonfloating
levels: floating tablets with 10% or 20% sodium bicarbonate
independent_variables.section: Swelling and erosion studies; In vitro drug release; Figure 7–8, 11; Table 7. [file:1]

dependent_variables_and_responses: Moisture content and Carr’s index of powders/granules. [file:1]
Tablet hardness, friability, weight uniformity, drug content uniformity. [file:1]
Floating lag time and total floating duration. [file:1]
Swelling (dissolution medium uptake %) and mass loss %. [file:1]
Dissolution profile (% drug released vs time). [file:1]
Korsmeyer–Peppas kinetics: n, Kp, R2 (release mechanism/kinetics). [file:1]

tests: post hoc multiple comparisons when required
statistical_analysis.sections: Statistical analysis; in-text P-values in results tables/figures. [file:1]

powder_pre_processing.sieving: All powders passed through 180 µm sieve; sodium alginate used with particle size ≤350 µm. [file:1]
powder_pre_processing.mixing: Turbula mixer at 60 rpm for 10 min. [file:1]

wet_granulation.procedure: Powder mixtures wetted with 0.5% w/w water, mixed 10 min, passed through 1000 µm sieve, dried at 60°C overnight, then sized to ≤853 µm. [file:1]

flowability_tests.moisture_content: Measured with Mettler Toledo HG53 Halogen Moisture Analyzer; results in Table 2. [file:1]
flowability_tests.carr_index_equation: CI = (tapped density − bulk density) / tapped density × 100%. [file:1]

uv_vis_calibration.figure: Calibration curve shown in Figure 1 (mean ± SD, small SD). [file:1]

formulation_details.note_on_F1A_F1B_F2A_F2B: The article defines 023_F1 vs 023_F2 by sodium bicarbonate level and uses hardness levels A (50–54 N) and B (54–59 N); it does not explicitly label batches as 023_F1(A), 023_F1(B), 023_F2(A), 023_F2(B) but those combinations can be inferred (023_F1 or 023_F2 at hardness A or B, and origin = powder mixture or granules). No separate compositions by A/B, only hardness differences. [file:1]

023_F2.note: Higher tablet weight due to 20% sodium bicarbonate. [file:1]

F1_A_granules.porosity_percent: see Figure 6 (after granulation, lower than before; exact numeric not in text). [file:1]

moisture_content_and_carr_index.replicates: Mean ± SD of three determinations; P-values in Table 2 show significant improvement in flow (CI decreased from poor to fair). [file:1]

mass_distribution.note: Tablet weight uniformity data (means and % deviation) are given, but distribution tables for pre-compression blend mass distribution are not separately tabulated. [file:1]

pre_compression_size.sieving: Powders: 180 µm, alginate ≤350 µm; granules collected at ≤853 µm. [file:1]

levels: A: 50–54 N (used for floating/nonfloating granule tablets)
C: 59–64 N (achieved only with powder mixture tablets)

FTIR_measurements.instrument: Perkin Elmer FT-IR system Spectrum BX series (UK)
FTIR_measurements.sample_prep: Few mg of sample placed on sample stage; compressed by twisting arm top clockwise. [file:1]
FTIR_measurements.compatibility_conclusion: Characteristic drug peaks present at similar wavenumbers in 023_F1/023_F2 powder and granule spectra → no evidence of incompatibility with excipients. [file:1]

compression.machine: Single-punch tableting machine Type 3 (Manesty Machines Ltd, UK)
groups: Automatically pressed tablets from granules (primary formulations for floating, dissolution, swelling/erosion).
Automatically pressed tablets from granules with 0% sodium bicarbonate (nonfloating control).
Manually pressed tablets from powder mixtures (powder fed manually to die) for density/floating/dissolution comparisons.

F1_A_granules.compliance: Within BP limits for friability, weight uniformity, and content uniformity. [file:1]

consolidation_index.note: A separate 'consolidation index' is not explicitly defined or reported; porosity and density changes with hardness and granulation are provided instead. [file:1]

drug_content_assay.procedure: Single tablet crushed; appropriate amount dispersed in 100 mL 0.1 N HCl at 37°C ± 0.5°C using shaking water bath; filtered through 0.45 µm cellulose acetate membrane; assayed by UV at 274 nm with calibration curve. [file:1]

granulation_process.equipment: Kenwood ChefKneader; drying oven (SciQuip Ltd, UK)

compression_process.device: Single-punch tableting machine Type 3 (Manesty Machines Ltd, UK)
compression_process.pressure: Not given numerically; controlled indirectly via hardness levels A/B/C. [file:1]
compression_process.coating: No coating applied; uncoated matrix tablets.

density_and_porosity.true_density_measurement: Multipycnometer MVP-D160-E (Quantachrome Instruments, USA), helium pressure 17 psi, ~1.8 g sample, average of five measurements. [file:1]

dissolution_conditions.apparatus: USP Apparatus II (paddle) – Erweka GmbH, Germany
dissolution_conditions.sink_conditions_rationale: Pentoxifylline solubility 191 mg/mL; 60 mg in 900 mL ensures sink conditions. [file:1]

release_profiles_summary.before_granulation: Increasing hardness (A→B) significantly decreases drug release for both 023_F1 and 023_F2 powder tablets; higher sodium bicarbonate (20%) increases release rate for powder tablets due to more pores. [file:1]
release_profiles_summary.after_granulation: Granulation generally decreases release rate (delayed medium penetration due to reduced porosity); for granule tablets 023_F2 (20% NaHCO3) shows lower release rate than 023_F1, corresponding to lower swelling and prolonged floating. [file:1]

release_kinetics_and_mechanism.model: Korsmeyer–Peppas power law: Qt/Q∞ = Kp * t^n (first 60% of release). [file:1]

missing_sets.F2_A_powder: Korsmeyer–Peppas parameters not provided due to insufficient data points below 60% release. [file:1]

release_kinetics_and_mechanism.qualitative_mechanism: Granulation tends to shift mechanism from Fickian to non-Fickian; higher sodium bicarbonate in granule tablets decreases Kp while slightly increasing n (greater role of polymer relaxation/erosion). [file:1]

order_of_kinetics.note: The article does not explicitly fit zero-order and first-order models across all formulations; only Korsmeyer–Peppas modeling is systematically reported. Therefore, zero-order and first-order rate constants for each batch are not available. [file:1]

pH_and_enzyme_influence.note: All in vitro release tests performed in 0.1 N HCl (pH 1.2) without enzymes; pH or enzyme effects are discussed conceptually only in the introduction (general comments about intestinal/colonic environments), not experimentally evaluated. [file:1]

swelling_and_erosion.conditions: Granule tablets, hardness levels A and B; 0.1 N HCl. [file:1]
swelling_and_erosion.swelling_DMU_percent: Measured at 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24 h; all tablets show continuous increase up to 12 h. 023_F2 formulations (20% NaHCO3) exhibit significantly higher DMU than 023_F1 at same hardness; nonfloating tablets behave similarly to F1. [file:1]
swelling_and_erosion.mass_loss_percent: Gradually increases to ~50% of original mass at 24 h for all; 023_F2 formulations show significantly higher mass loss than 023_F1 due to greater effervescence; nonfloating tablets have lowest mass loss. [file:1]
swelling_and_erosion.lag_time_floating_vs_swelling: Prolonged floating (especially 023_F2) reduces effective surface area in contact with medium, leading to lower swelling for 023_F2 vs 023_F1 despite higher NaHCO3. [file:1]

disintegration_time.note: Disintegration time is not reported; these are designed as sustained-release floating matrices, not fast-disintegrating tablets. [file:1]

stability.note: No long-term stability studies (e.g., ICH conditions) or stability data are reported. [file:1]

water_uptake_and_swelling_index.lag_time_minutes: Floating lag time reported (see above); not a classic swelling lag time but onset of buoyancy.
water_uptake_and_swelling_index.total_floating_time_hours: See floating duration for each batch.
water_uptake_and_swelling_index.swelling_index_percent: Expressed as % dissolution medium uptake; individual numeric values at each time point per batch are shown in Figure 7 but not tabulated. [file:1]

studies: Swelling and erosion (DMU, mass loss) in 0.1 N HCl. [file:1]
Floating lag time and duration during dissolution. [file:1]
Dissolution profiles of all formulations (023_F1, 023_F2, nonfloating). [file:1]
Korsmeyer–Peppas modeling for mechanism (n, Kp, R2). [file:1]

examples: Granulation significantly decreases moisture content and Carr’s index (P < 0.05). [file:1]
Granulation significantly reduces tablet apparent density for most formulations, except 023_F2 at hardness B where effect is nonsignificant. [file:1]
Hardness increase significantly decreases drug release for powder tablets (P < 0.05), but effect is nonsignificant for granule tablets. [file:1]

in_vivo.note: No in vivo studies reported for these formulations; in vivo concepts are only mentioned in the introduction via literature. [file:1]
